data.title: U.S. Consumer Sentiment Recovers to 6-Month High as Inflation Expectations Dip
data.description:
The Index of Consumer Sentiment rose to 56.6 in February from 56.4 in January, marking a six-month high. However, the index is still down by 12.5% year-over-year, with over 40% of consumers citing high prices eroding their personal finances as a key concern for the seventh consecutive month.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

In addition, sentiment among high-income consumers and those with significant stock holdings remains stronger than among lower-income consumers and those without stock market exposure.

"With their much stronger income prospects and investment portfolios, wealthier and higher-income consumers feel better insulated from any possible risks to the economy," said Surveys of Consumers Director Joanne Hsu.

Year-Ahead Inflation Expectations Fall to 13-Month Low

Meanwhile, year-ahead inflation expectations fell to 3.4%, the lowest since January 2025 and down from 4% during the prior month. Long-run inflation expectations remained unchanged at 3.3% and are still above the range of 2.8% to 3.2% in 2024.

Both year-ahead and long-run expectations remain above December's Core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) index reading of 3% announced by the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) this morning. The index tracks inflation based on household consumption of goods and services, excluding volatile food and energy prices, and is the Fed's preferred gauge of inflation.

data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: IBM and RIKEN Achieve a Major Quantum-Supercomputing Milestone
data.description:
Scientists at RIKEN and IBM (IBM) have achieved a major milestone by combining a powerful supercomputer with a quantum computer to solve a complex chemistry problem. More specifically, they connected the Fugaku supercomputer with IBM's Quantum Heron processor to study iron-sulfur molecules, which play important roles in biology and chemistry. As a result, this became the largest and most accurate chemistry experiment ever run on a quantum computer. Importantly, this was not two machines working separately, but rather both systems operating together in a tightly coordinated way.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Instead of running one calculation after another, the supercomputer and quantum processor continuously passed information back and forth in a closed loop. Unsurprisingly, this required building a new workflow to make sure that neither system sat idle, since both are extremely expensive and powerful machines. Fugaku alone contains nearly 159,000 chips with 48 cores each and was once the fastest computer in the world. By carefully assigning tasks between the two systems, the team minimized wasted time and showed that quantum and classical computers can efficiently work side-by-side, even at a massive scale.

To solve the problem, researchers used an approach called sample-based quantum diagonalization. In simple terms, the quantum computer handled the hardest part, exploring many possible electron arrangements, while the classical supercomputer refined those results. Because of this, the outcome was far more accurate than previous quantum attempts and comparable to advanced classical methods. Looking ahead, the team plans to add GPUs to make the process even faster.

Is IBM a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Moderate Buy consensus rating on IBM stock based on 12 Buys, five Holds, and one Sell assigned in the past three months, as indicated by the graphic below. Furthermore, the average IBM price target of $337.53 per share implies 31.1% upside potential.
data.author: Vince Condarcuri

data.title: Stock Market Today: SPY, QQQ Rally as Trump Announces 10% Global Tariff Hike in Response to SCOTUS Ruling
data.description:
The S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) closed Friday on higher ground as investors digested a tariff ruling from the Supreme Court and President Trump's response to the decision.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

In a 6-3 ruling, the Supreme Court struck down the legality of Trump's tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). SCOTUS didn't provide guidance on potential tariff refunds, although it warned that the process could turn out to be a "'mess,' as was acknowledged at oral
arguments." The refunds could amount to $175 billion, according to an estimate released by Penn-Wharton Budget Model economists this morning.

Trump criticized the ruling and announced that he would sign an executive order to impose a new 10% global tariff under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974. "Today I will sign an Order to impose a 10% GLOBAL TARIFF, under Section 122, over and above our normal TARIFFS already being charged," he said in a Truth Social post. Section 122 allows the president to impose tariffs of up to 15% for a maximum of 150 days in response to trade deficits or other trade disruptions. The duration can be extended with congressional approval.

Trump also stated that he would begin Section 301 investigations. The statute gives the U.S. Trade Representative authority to investigate unfair trade practices and permits the president to impose tariffs or other penalties based on those findings. Tariffs under Section 301 carry no rate limit and can last up to eight years.

"In order to protect our Country, a President can actually charge more TARIFFS than I was charging in the past under the various other TARIFF authorities, which have also been confirmed, and fully allowed," Trump said.

Elsewhere, the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) announced that the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Index rose by 2.9% year-over-year in December, the highest rate since March 2024 and above the estimate of 2.8%. Core PCE, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, increased by 3% from the prior year, the highest since February 2025. Core PCE is the Fed's preferred gauge of inflation and remains above its target of 2%.

Furthermore, gross domestic product (GDP) growth slowed significantly in the fourth quarter, falling to 1.4% from 4.4% in the third quarter and missing the consensus estimate of 3.0%. Consumer spending and investment contributed to the growth and were partially offset by the effects of the government shutdown and declining exports.

The S&P 500 (SPX) closed with a 0.69% gain, while the Nasdaq 100 (NDX) returned 0.87%.

data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: Trump Rolls Out '10% Global Tariff' with Section 301 Investigations Underway
data.description:
On Friday, the Supreme Court ruled to strike down President Trump's tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). However, Trump is moving quickly to implement tariffs using other legal measures.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

"Today I will sign an Order to impose a 10% GLOBAL TARIFF, under Section 122, over and above our normal TARIFFS already being charged," Trump said in a Truth Social post.

Trump Turns to Section 122 and Section 301 to Impose Tariffs

Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 allows the president to impose tariffs of up to 15% for a maximum of 150 days, extendable with congressional approval. The statute can be used to address trade deficits, does not require a federal investigation, and has never been invoked by a president until now.

Trump added that he would begin the process of Section 301 investigations. Section 301, under the same trade act as Section 122, authorizes the U.S. Trade Representative to investigate unfair trade practices and the president to impose tariffs or other trade penalties based on those findings.

data.title: "Netflix Does Not Need this Deal": Netflix Stock (NASDAQ:NFLX) Notches Up as Warner Merger Looks Shaky
data.description:
Streaming giant Netflix (NFLX) may be on the verge of taking over Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). There are doubts, however, that that deal will actually reach fruition. But even if the deal does not conclude successfully, Netflix will likely survive regardless. That is the word from several analysts, and it was encouraging enough to shareholders to give Netflix an extra 2% in the closing minutes of Friday's trading.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Wedbush analysts—led by Alicia Reese, who has a four-star rating on TipRanks—left their Buy rating in place, reports noted, and left their price target of $115 per share quite intact. Given that that is nearly 50% over Netflix's current trading price, there should be a clear reason why it commands such a premium.

The Wedbush analysts made it clear: Netflix "…does not need this deal." The analysts further noted,"Its business is entirely healthy on its own, with a burgeoning global advertising business." This is true, of course; a Netflix with Warner in its stable gets access to an incredible library of content and the ability to make much, much more. It dovetails well with Netflix's Fort Monmouth plans, and gives Netflix production ability on two coasts. But a Netflix without Warner is hardly ruined.

Exotic Stop-Motion, Aisle Three

Meanwhile, Netflix also landed an unusual feather in its cap, becoming the streaming home of the first-ever stop-motion film made in Mexico. Known as I Am Frankelda, it follows the story of a 19th century writer known simply as Frankelda. Frankelda's work is unusually dark, and being the 19th century, her ability to tell these stories is, well, restricted. But when Frankelda finds herself stuffed into her own subconscious, the monsters she birthed therein are all there…and waiting.

The film will actually be competing for an Annie Award. The event takes place this weekend, and regardless of the event's outcome, the film will still have its shot on Netflix. Guillermo del Toro, who served as the directors' mentor, declared the film "…a landmark in Mexican Stop-Motion animation and a true triumph of vision, tenacity and love for the craft."

Is Netflix Stock a Good Buy Right Now?

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Moderate Buy consensus rating on NFLX stock based on 27 Buys, eight Holds, and one Sell assigned in the past three months, as indicated by the graphic below. After a 23.24% loss in its share price over the past year, the average NFLX price target of $114.52 per share implies 45.62% upside potential.
data.author: Steve Anderson

data.title: Why Is IREN Stock Down Today and Why One Investor Says 'Ignore the Noise'
data.description:
IREN (NASDAQ:IREN) shares tumbled 7% today after a report that lenders were hesitant to back a $4 billion data-center financing deal tied to CoreWeave, raising questions about how some of the smaller AI infrastructure players will fund rapid expansion. That development rippled through the group, with investors reassessing risk and reallocating toward larger, more established tech names, leaving IREN under pressure.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

AI-related concerns have been pressuring IREN's share price for a while, with the stock down 36% since the end of January, as massive hyperscaler capex spending has unsettled investors.

Needless to say, the company is going full-throttle into the AI-verse, and its recent earnings report provided another demonstration of its transition from Bitcoin miner to provider of AI compute power.

Among its points of pride, IREN management highlighted the $3.6 billion in financing it has secured to cover 95% of the GPU-related capex required to fulfill its $9.7 billion AI contract with Microsoft. The company also said it is making solid progress bringing in both new and existing GPUs online. By the numbers, IREN expects to deploy 140,000 GPUs by year-end, which would support $3.4 billion in annual run-rate revenue.

However, much of the chatter surrounding IREN has been focused on its recent revenue miss. IREN's $184.7 million in sales in the last quarter missed by close to $40 million, and also represented a decline from the $240.3 million it delivered in Q1 FY 2026. Management chalked this up to lower Bitcoin mining revenue, as IREN dedicates its compute power to run AI workloads.

One investor, known by the pseudonym Pythia Research, believes that the recent dip reflects a profound misunderstanding of the company and the investment case.

"The narrative is not broken, but rather the monetization assumption is now clearly front-loaded on capital expenditures and back-loaded on revenues," explains the 5-star investor.

Furthermore, Pythia points out that IREN sits at the nexus of two different stories: Bitcoin prices and the AI infrastructure build-out. Unfortunately for IREN, both of these are negative at the moment. Despite the company's transition away from Bitcoin mining, it still has plenty of exposure. Therefore, when cryptocurrency prices are volatile and markets are uneasy about AI capex, it shouldn't be a surprise that the high-beta stock is reacting "violently."

Pythia prefers to take a longer-term view, one where the company's AI bet surpasses its Bitcoin mining business. The investor appreciates that there's still a long way to go, as Bitcoin still drives the "lion's share" of sales. "Mining is the past – capacity is the thesis," emphasizes Pythia.

Looking ahead, the investor will be laser-focused on how IREN is able to progress toward its goal of hundreds of millions in annual AI revenues. Specifically, Pythia wants to see evidence that IREN is able to reduce its reliance on mining cash flows.

The investor is also eager to watch the pace at which IREN converts secured capacity into contracts. It has the power, Pythia adds, now it needs the customer contracts.

"This is no longer a stock to buy based on a momentum trade. It's a stock to buy based on execution," concludes Pythia, who rates IREN a Strong Buy. (To watch Pythia Research's track record, click here)

Wall Street isn't scared away by the recent dip either. With 9 Buys, 2 Holds, and 1 Sell, IREN carries a Moderate Buy consensus rating. Its 12-month average price target of $79.78 points to an upside of ~91%. (See IREN stock forecast)
data.author: Gabe Ross

data.title: Ahead of Q4 Earnings, Let's Look at Who Owns Hims & Hers Stock (HIMS)
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) is set to report its fourth-quarter 2025 results on February 23. Year-to-date, HIMS stock is down by 51% amid rising competition in the telehealth space, concerns over slowing growth, and legal troubles. In this article, we take a closer look at who owns HIMS stock. Looking at both institutional investors and company insiders, Hims & Hers' ownership gives a useful view of where confidence and caution lie ahead of earnings.

For context, Hims & Hers Health provides accessible healthcare products and services, including prescription medications, wellness products, and online medical consultations.

Who Owns HIMS Stock?

According to TipRanks' Ownership Tool, public companies and individual investors own 46.9% of HIMS. They are followed by mutual funds, ETFs, insiders, and other institutional investors, at 23.67%, 21.29%, 5.10%, and 3.02%, respectively.

Among institutional investors, Vanguard is HIMS' largest shareholder with about 8.5% of the shares. iShares comes second with a 7.3% stake, followed by Vanguard Index Funds at around 6.8%.

On the ETF side, the iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH) owns about 3.07% of HIMS, the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) holds 2.9%, and the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) has 2.33%.

What to Expect from HIMS' Q4 Results?

In terms of financial results, analysts expect Hims & Hers to post adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of $0.04, much lower than the prior year's figure of $0.11. Meanwhile, sales are forecast to jump 28.7% year-over-year to $619.23 million.

Is HIMS Stock a Good Buy Right Now? 

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Hold consensus rating on HIMS stock based on three Buys, six Holds, and two Sells assigned in the past three months. The average stock price target of HIMS is $32.39 per share, which implies an upside of over 100%.
data.author: Kirti Tak
